content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll explore the codebase to understand its structure before writing the CLAUDE.md.

input.prompt:
Explore the codebase at X:\01_Edmb\Clockhelp to understand its architecture for writing a CLAUDE.md file. I need:

1. Read package.json to understand scripts, dependencies, and project type
2. Read any README.md at the root
3. Look at the top-level directory structure
4. Read the main entry point files (e.g., main.js, index.js, electron/main.js or similar)
5. Read src/ directory structure and key files (especially any router/App component)
6. Read electron/ directory files
7. Check for any config files: .eslintrc, vite.config.js, tailwind.config.js, etc.
8. Read electron/database.js to understand the data layer
9. Check for any existing CLAUDE.md or cursor rules

Return a thorough summary covering: tech stack, build/dev/test commands, architecture (how Electron + React are connected, IPC patterns, data flow), key files and their roles.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me create a detailed summary document.

## Architecture Summary for Clockhelp

I've completed a thorough exploration of the codebase. Here's a comprehensive summary covering the tech stack, architecture, and key files:

---

### TECH STACK

**Frontend:**
- React 18.2.0 + Vite 5.0.8 (dev server on port 5173)
- TailwindCSS 3.4.0 for styling with custom blue/dark color palette
- Zustand 4.4.7 for state management
- Lucide React 0.300.0 for icons
- date-fns 3.0.0 for date utilities

**Desktop & Main Process:**
- Electron 28 (entry: electron/main.js)
- sql.js 1.8.0 (pure JavaScript SQLite, no native compilation)
- electron-store 8.1.0 for basic persistence
- Context Bridge for secure IPC communication

**Build & Development:**
- Vite for React bundling
- electron-builder for executable packaging (NSIS installer for Windows)
- concurrently + wait-on for dev orchestration

**Commands:**
- `npm run dev` - Start Vite dev server
- `npm run electron:dev` - Run Electron with hot reload (waits for Vite)
- `npm run build` - Vite production build
- `npm run electron:build` - Create Windows installer

---

### PROJECT STRUCTURE

```
clockhelp/
├── electron/                    # Main process (Node.js)
│   ├── main.js                 # App orchestration, IPC setup, shortcuts
│   ├── database.js             # sql.js wrapper, schema, save/load
│   ├── timer.js                # Timer state machine & persistence
│   ├── windows.js              # Main window & floating widget
│   ├── preload.cjs             # Context bridge (IPC API exposure)
│   ├── idle.js                 # System idle detection
│   ├── tray.js                 # System tray integration
│   └── reminders.js            # Scheduled task reminders
│
├── src/                         # React renderer process
│   ├── main.jsx                # React entry point
│   ├── App.jsx                 # Router logic (main vs widget)
│   ├── index.css               # TailwindCSS imports
│   ├── components/             # React UI components
│   │   ├── MainWindow.jsx      # Tab container
│   │   ├── Dashboard.jsx       # Timer UI & quick task selection
│   │   ├── FloatingWidget.jsx  # Compact always-on-top timer
│   │   ├── CustomerManager.jsx # CRUD for customers
│   │   ├── ProjectManager.jsx  # CRUD for projects
│   │   ├── TaskManager.jsx     # CRUD for tasks
│   │   ├── WeeklyReport.jsx    # Timesheet + CSV export
│   │   ├── Settings.jsx        # Config UI
│   │   ├── ManualEntry.jsx     # Add/edit time entries
│   │   ├── Schedule.jsx        # Task scheduling
│   │   └── IdleModal.jsx       # Idle confirmation dialog
│   ├── stores/                 # Zustand state
│   │   ├── dataStore.js        # CRUD actions via IPC
│   │   ├── timerStore.js       # Timer state + IPC listeners
│   │   ├── settingsStore.js    # Settings management
│   │   └── scheduleStore.js    # Scheduled tasks
│   └── utils/                  # Helper functions
│
├── dist/                        # Vite build output (included in package)
├── index.html                  # HTML entry point
├── vite.config.js              # Vite config with @ alias
├── tailwind.config.js          # TailwindCSS theme
├── postcss.config.js           # PostCSS + Tailwind
└── package.json                # Dependencies & scripts
```

---

### ARCHITECTURE & DATA FLOW

**Electron + React Connection:**

1. **Main Process (electron/main.js)**
   - Initializes database, tray, floating widget on app startup
   - Restores active timer if app was closed mid-tracking
   - Registers 3 global keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+Shift+T/L/W)
   - Creates 2 windows: main window + floating widget (both use same React app, differentiated by hash)

2. **Window Management (electron/windows.js)**
   - Main window: 800×800, dev tools enabled in dev mode
   - Floating widget: 350×200, frameless, always-on-top, resizable
   - Both load from Vite dev server (http://localhost:5173) or dist/index.html in production
   - Widget identified by `window.location.hash === '#/widget'` in App.jsx

3. **IPC Bridge (electron/preload.cjs)**
   - Secure context bridge exposes `window.electronAPI` to renderer
   - Database: dbQuery, dbRun, dbGet, dbAll
   - Timer: startTimer, stopTimer, pauseTimer, resumeTimer, getTimerState
   - Lunch breaks: startLunchBreak, stopLunchBreak
   - Events: onTimerTick, onTimerState, onIdleDetected, onUpcomingReminders, onTodaySchedule
   - Window ops: closeWindow, minimizeWindow, toggleWidget, resizeWidget
   - Settings: setAutoLaunch
   - Schedules: getScheduledTasks, add/update/deleteScheduledTask

4. **Data Flow: IPC Handlers (electron/main.js)**
   ```
   Renderer (React) → window.electronAPI.xxx → ipcMain.handle/on → Main Process Logic
   Main Process → BrowserWindow.send() → window.electronAPI.onXxx listener → Store action
   ```

---

### DATABASE ARCHITECTURE

**sql.js Integration (electron/database.js)**
- Pure JavaScript SQLite (sql.js), no native compilation required
- Loaded from node_modules/sql.js/dist/sql-wasm.wasm
- Stored in %APPDATA%\clockhelp\clockhelp.db
- Auto-saves every 30 seconds + debounced saves (1s) after mutations
- Rolling backups: 3 versions (.backup.1/2/3) to protect against corruption
- Atomic writes: export to .tmp file, verify SQLite header, then rotate & rename

**Schema:**
```sql
customers (id, name, active, created_at)
projects (id, customer_id, name, active, created_at)
tasks (id, project_id, name, active, created_at)
time_entries (id, task_id, start_time, end_time, duration_seconds, is_lunch_break, notes, created_at)
  - task_id can be NULL (for lunch breaks)
  - Indexes on task_id, start_time
settings (key, value)
  - Keys: dailyBudgetHours, lunchDurationMinutes, idleThresholdMinutes, autoPauseOnIdle, etc.
scheduled_tasks (id, task_id, day_of_week, specific_date, start_time, end_time, recurrence_type, recurrence_pattern, is_active, created_at)
```

**Helper Functions:**
- query(sql, params) → Returns raw result object with columns & values
- run(sql, params) → Executes, auto-saves, returns {success: true}
- get(sql, params) → Returns first row as object or null
- all(sql, params) → Returns array of row objects

---

### TIMER STATE MACHINE (electron/timer.js)

**Timer State Object:**
```js
{
  isActive,      // boolean
  isPaused,      // boolean
  taskId,        // number | null
  taskName,      // string
  startTime,     // Date
  pausedTime,    // Date | null
  elapsedSeconds,// number (calculated from startTime)
  entryId        // number (time_entries row id)
}
```

**Timer Flow:**
1. `startTimer(taskId)` → Creates time_entries row, starts 1s interval tick
2. Tick calculates `elapsedSeconds = (now - startTime) / 1000` for accuracy
3. Broadcasts via `BrowserWindow.send('timer:tick')` every 1 second
4. `stopTimer()` → Records end_time & duration, clears state
5. `pauseTimer()` / `resumeTimer()` → Just toggles isPaused flag
6. `restoreTimer()` → Checks for active entries on startup, recalculates elapsed time

**Persistence:**
- Active timer saved to settings table as JSON (key='active_timer')
- On app restart, restoreTimer() queries for entries with `end_time IS NULL`
- Elapsed time recalculated from saved startTime to current time

---

### STATE MANAGEMENT (Zustand Stores)

**timerStore.js:**
- Mirrors main process timer state
- Listeners for 'timer:tick' and 'timer:state' events auto-update store
- Actions call electronAPI (IPC) → main process → return results

**dataStore.js:**
- CRUD actions for customers, projects, tasks, time entries
- All queries via window.electronAPI.dbAll/dbRun/dbGet
- loadAll() → Promise.all() for parallel loading
- Methods track loading/error state

**settingsStore.js & scheduleStore.js:**
- Similar pattern: query via IPC, update local state

---

### KEY FILES & ROLES

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| electron/main.js | App lifecycle, IPC handlers, global shortcuts, window creation |
| electron/database.js | sql.js init, schema, auto-save, CRUD wrappers |
| electron/timer.js | Timer state machine, interval tick, broadcasts |
| electron/windows.js | Window creation (main + widget), Vite/dist loading |
| electron/preload.cjs | Context bridge exposing electronAPI |
| electron/idle.js | powerMonitor polling every 10s, broadcasts idle:detected |
| src/App.jsx | Routes main window vs floating widget by URL hash |
| src/main.jsx | React entry point |
| src/stores/dataStore.js | CRUD + loading state for all entities |
| src/stores/timerStore.js | Timer UI state + IPC listeners |
| src/components/Dashboard.jsx | Timer controls, recent tasks, daily progress |
| src/components/MainWindow.jsx | Tab navigation container |
| src/components/FloatingWidget.jsx | Compact timer for widget window |
| vite.config.js | @ path alias, dev server port 5173 |
| postcss.config.js | Tailwind processing |

---

### IMPORTANT PATTERNS

**IPC Communication Pattern:**
- All renderer→main calls use `ipcMain.handle()` (promise-based)
- Main→renderer broadcasts use `BrowserWindow.send()` + `ipcRenderer.on()`
- Preload carefully exposes only needed methods, maintains context isolation

**Timer Accuracy:**
- Elapsed time calculated from `startTime`, not accumulated, prevents drift
- Each tick: `elapsedSeconds = floor((now - startTime) / 1000)`

**Database Safety:**
- Exports verified before save (SQLite header check)
- Temp file write first, then atomic rename
- Multiple backups protect against corruption

**App Startup Sequence:**
1. Electron ready → initDatabase()
2. createFloatingWidget() (always visible)
3. createTray()
4. restoreTimer() (if active entry exists)
5. startIdleDetection()
6. startReminderEngine()
7. registerGlobalShortcuts()

---

### NOTES FOR CLAUDE.md

This codebase demonstrates:
- **Clean separation:** Electron main/renderer boundary with preload isolation
- **State synchronization:** Zustand stores listen to IPC events
- **Timer accuracy:** Calculation-based, not accumulative
- **Desktop features:** System tray, floating widget, global shortcuts, idle detection
- **Database resilience:** sql.js with atomic saves, backups, corruption recovery
- **Configuration:** TailwindCSS for theme consistency, path aliases in Vite
- **TypeScript ready:** Uses ES modules (type: "module"), but currently plain JavaScript

There is **no existing CLAUDE.md file** in the repo—ready to be created.

### NOTES FOR CLAUDE.md

This codebase demonstrates:
- **Clean separation:** Electron main/renderer boundary with preload isolation
- **State synchronization:** Zustand stores listen to IPC events
- **Timer accuracy:** Calculation-based, not accumulative
- **Desktop features:** System tray, floating widget, global shortcuts, idle detection
- **Database resilience:** sql.js with atomic saves, backups, corruption recovery
- **Configuration:** TailwindCSS for theme consistency, path aliases in Vite
- **TypeScript ready:** Uses ES modules (type: "module"), but currently plain JavaScript

There is **no existing CLAUDE.md file** in the repo—ready to be created.
Now I'll write the CLAUDE.md file.

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Commands

```bash
npm run electron:dev   # Dev: starts Vite (port 5173) then Electron with hot reload
npm run dev            # Vite renderer only (no Electron)
npm run build          # Vite production build → dist/
npm run electron:build # Package Windows installer (NSIS) via electron-builder
```

No test suite is configured. No linter config present.

## Architecture

**Clockhelp** is a time-tracking desktop app built with Electron 28 + React 18 (Vite) + sql.js (pure-JS SQLite).

### Process boundary

Two OS windows are created, both loading the same React app from Vite dev server or `dist/index.html`:
- **Main window** — full UI with tabs
- **Floating widget** — frameless, always-on-top, 350×200 — identified by `window.location.hash === '#/widget'` in `src/App.jsx`

The React renderer never touches Node.js directly. `electron/preload.cjs` uses `contextBridge` to expose `window.electronAPI`, which wraps `ipcRenderer.invoke`/`ipcRenderer.on` calls.

### IPC pattern

```
Renderer → window.electronAPI.xxx()
         → ipcMain.handle / ipcMain.on  (electron/main.js registers all handlers)
         → main-process logic (timer.js, database.js, etc.)

Main process → BrowserWindow.send('event:name', payload)
             → window.electronAPI.onXxx(cb) listener
             → Zustand store update
```

### Timer (electron/timer.js)

State machine tracking `{isActive, isPaused, taskId, startTime, elapsedSeconds, entryId}`. Elapsed time is always **calculated** from `startTime` (not accumulated) to avoid drift. Ticks every 1 s via `setInterval`, broadcasts `timer:tick` to all windows. Active timer is persisted to the `settings` table (key `active_timer`) as JSON and restored on startup by scanning for `time_entries` rows with `end_time IS NULL`.

### Database (electron/database.js)

- sql.js loaded with WASM from `node_modules/sql.js/dist/sql-wasm.wasm`
- DB file at `%APPDATA%\clockhelp\clockhelp.db`
- Auto-saves every 30 s + debounced 1 s save after every mutation
- Atomic writes: export → `.tmp` → verify SQLite header → rotate 3 backups → rename
- Helper API: `query(sql, params)`, `run(sql, params)`, `get(sql, params)`, `all(sql, params)`

**Schema tables:** `customers`, `projects`, `tasks`, `time_entries` (task_id nullable for lunch breaks), `settings` (key/value), `scheduled_tasks`

### State management (src/stores/)

Four Zustand stores mirror main-process state:
- `timerStore.js` — registers `timer:tick` / `timer:state` IPC listeners on init
- `dataStore.js` — CRUD via `dbAll`/`dbRun`/`dbGet`, `loadAll()` runs fetches in parallel
- `settingsStore.js` / `scheduleStore.js` — same IPC pattern

### App startup sequence

`electron/main.js` `app.whenReady()`:
1. `initDatabase()` → schema migration
2. `createFloatingWidget()` (always visible)
3. `createTray()`
4. `restoreTimer()` (reopens active entries from DB)
5. `startIdleDetection()` (polls `powerMonitor` every 10 s)
6. `startReminderEngine()` (scheduled-task alerts)
7. Register global shortcuts: Ctrl+Shift+T (start/stop), Ctrl+Shift+L (lunch), Ctrl+Shift+W (toggle widget)

### Key files

| Path | Role |
|---|---|
| `electron/main.js` | App lifecycle, all IPC handlers, global shortcuts |
| `electron/preload.cjs` | Context bridge — defines the full `electronAPI` surface |
| `electron/database.js` | sql.js init, schema, atomic save, CRUD helpers |
| `electron/timer.js` | Timer state machine + tick broadcast |
| `electron/windows.js` | Window creation (main + widget) |
| `electron/idle.js` | Idle detection via `powerMonitor` |
| `src/App.jsx` | Routes main window vs widget by `window.location.hash` |
| `src/stores/timerStore.js` | Timer UI state + IPC event listeners |
| `src/stores/dataStore.js` | Entity CRUD + loading state |
| `src/components/Dashboard.jsx` | Timer controls, task picker, daily progress |
| `src/components/FloatingWidget.jsx` | Compact always-on-top timer UI |
| `vite.config.js` | `@` alias → `src/`, dev server port 5173 |

### Styling

TailwindCSS 3 with a custom palette (`blue-*` accents, dark backgrounds). All class names are in JSX; no CSS modules. The `@` import alias resolves to `src/`.

`CLAUDE.md` created at the repo root. It covers:

- **Commands** for dev, build, and packaging
- **Architecture** — the two-window Electron setup and how they're differentiated
- **IPC pattern** — how renderer talks to main and vice versa
- **Timer** — the calculation-based elapsed-time approach and persistence strategy

lastPrompt: I need in manual entry, once i tipe the start time, it copies to the end time, so is more simple to modify it, also manual entry should have a password protection, so i am the only to add manual entri…

aiTitle: Add auto-copy start time and password protection for manual entries

content.text:
Both files look correct. Here's what was implemented:

---

**Feature 1 — Start time copies to end time**

In `ManualEntry.jsx:211`, the Start time `onChange` now calls both `setStartTime` and `setEndTime` with the same value. After you type a start time, end time immediately matches it — you only need to bump the end time forward. After saving an entry, the existing "advance for back-to-back entries" behavior (`setStartTime(endTime); setEndTime(nextEnd)`) is unaffected since that's programmatic, not triggered by the input.

---

**Feature 2 — Password protection**

**ManualEntry**: on mount it reads `manualEntryPassword` from the settings DB. If one exists, the tab shows a centered lock screen (lock icon + password input, Enter or button to unlock). Wrong password shakes with an error; correct password reveals the full form for the rest of the session.

**Settings**: a new "Manual Entry Protection" card lets you:
- Set a password (min 4 chars, with confirmation)
- Change it (requires current password first)
- Remove it (also requires current password)

The password is stored in the `settings` table under key `manualEntryPassword`. Go to Settings → set a password, and from that point employees hitting the Manual Entry tab will see the lock screen.

content: Added start-time auto-copy to end time and a password gate on Manual Entry, with password management in Settings. Test it by running npm run electron:dev. (disable recaps in /config)

aiTitle: Add auto-copy start time and password protection for manual entries

content.text: Let me verify the database.js has the `all` function signature I'm relying on for the import queries:

Good. Everything wires up correctly. Here's a summary of all the changes:

---

**Default password**
On first launch (no DB row yet), `ManualEntry` seeds `ThisIsPasswordManualEntry` into the settings table and locks the tab. If the owner removes the password in Settings, it writes an **empty string** instead of deleting the row — so on the next open, ManualEntry sees `row.value === ''` and stays unlocked (the default is never re-seeded once explicitly removed).

**Export**
- Available at two places: inside the lock screen AND in the Recent Entries header, so employees can export even without knowing the password.
- Queries all `time_entries` joined with customer/project/task and writes a JSON file via Electron's native save dialog.
- Format: `{ version, exportedAt, entries: [{ customer, project, task, startTime, endTime, durationSeconds, notes }] }`

**Import** (owner-only — only visible when unlocked)
- Opens a native file picker, reads the JSON, then for each entry: finds or creates customer → project → task (case-insensitive match), then inserts the time entry.
- Shows a green summary bar after import: `"Imported 47 entries · 2 new customer(s) · 3 new project(s)"`

**Overlap detection**
- `useMemo` runs an O(n²) check over the 20 most recent entries.
- Overlapping entries get a red background + border + `⚠ overlaps` label, and a badge in the header showing the count.

content: Building a time-tracking Electron app called Clockhelp. Just added export/import JSON data between employees and owner, default password protection for manual entries, and overlap highlighting. Ready for your next feature. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
That's already how it's built. In the current code, when ManualEntry is locked the export button is shown **above** the password prompt — employees can export their data without entering the password, then the lock screen stops them from adding or editing entries.

Here's the lock screen structure we already have:

```
ManualEntry (locked view)
├── [Export data] button  ← always visible, no password needed
└── 🔒 password prompt
```

Once they unlock (owner only), the full form + Import button appear.

No changes needed — it's already working this way.

content: Built password-protected Manual Entry with default password, start-time auto-copy to end, and JSON export/import with overlap detection. Next: test the app with `npm run electron:dev`. (disable recaps in /config)
